name: Curium
name_origin: Named in honor of Marie and Pierre Curie, pioneers of radioactivity research.

discovery.discoverer: Glenn T. Seaborg, Ralph A. James, Albert Ghiorso

occurrence.abundance_summary: Trace amounts have been detected in uranium ores from neutron capture processes, but for practical purposes curium is synthetic, produced in nuclear reactors by successive neutron capture on plutonium.
states: trace in uranium ores (synthetic in practice)

compounds.name: Curium(IV) oxide
compounds.summary: Black solid; one of the more stable +4 actinide oxides and a useful starting material for curium chemistry.

compounds.name: Curium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: Whitish solid that emits a characteristic glow due to self-radiation; the most thermodynamically stable curium oxide.

compounds.name: Curium(III) fluoride
compounds.summary: Insoluble white solid used as an intermediate in producing metallic curium by reduction with barium vapor.

uses: Alpha-particle X-ray spectrometers on Mars rovers and other planetary missions for elemental analysis (Cm-244)
Source of plutonium-238 via successive neutron capture and beta decay
Scientific research into transuranic chemistry and synthesis of heavier elements
Potential heat source for compact radioisotope thermoelectric generators
reactivity_summary: A silvery, malleable metal that is chemically reactive, more so than its lanthanide analog gadolinium. It tarnishes in air and reacts with acids. The +3 state dominates its chemistry, although +4 is accessible in solid compounds; curium glows pink-purple in the dark from intense self-radiation.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Curium-247 has the longest half-life (15.6 million years), but Cm-244 (half-life 18.1 years) and Cm-248 are the most commonly used in research and applications. Cm-244 is a strong alpha emitter used in spectrometers.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
